findings.description: Inverted session-expiry check: `if session.expires_at > datetime.utcnow()` deletes and rejects every still-valid (not-yet-expired) session as 'expired', while letting genuinely expired sessions fall through to `return session.user_id` — so live sessions are denied/destroyed on first use and expired sessions are accepted (correct condition is `<=`); cleanup_expired at line 148 uses the opposite, correct comparison, confirming the inversion.

SQL injection: email is interpolated into the query via f-string (`WHERE email = '{email}'`), allowing auth bypass/data exfiltration (e.g. `' OR '1'='1' -- ` returns a row, defeating the unknown-email->None logic); must use a parameterized query (security scope — defer detail to critic-security, flagged here as it directly subverts login logic).

Permanent lockout / dead constant: once `failed_attempts >= MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS` the account is rejected at line 70 BEFORE the password is checked, so the reset-to-0 at line 82 is unreachable for a locked user — a locked-out account can never unlock even with the correct password; LOCKOUT_MINUTES (line 17) is never referenced and no attempt-timestamp is stored, so the intended 15-minute time-based auto-unlock is entirely unimplemented.

rotate_token inherits the line-105 inversion: for a valid token validate_session returns None so rotation always fails, and for an actually-expired token validate_session returns a user_id, causing rotate_token to resurrect a dead session into a brand-new valid one (session-lifetime extension of expired credentials).

In-memory `_sessions` dict is per-process: in a multi-worker/multi-instance web service a token is only valid on the worker that created it (validate_session returns None elsewhere) and all sessions are lost on restart; combined with the line-105 bug, expired sessions are never evicted by validate_session, so memory grows unbounded unless cleanup_expired is called by an unshown scheduler.

find_user_by_email does `SELECT * FROM users`, materializes every row into User objects, then linear-scans in Python instead of filtering with a WHERE clause — O(n) full-table load per lookup that will not scale and also silently returns only the first match if email is non-unique.

Account-enumeration timing oracle: an unknown/inactive email returns immediately (line 59) without running the ~100k-iteration pbkdf2 hash, whereas a known email pays that cost, leaking valid-account existence via response timing (security scope — note in passing).

failed_attempts read at line 68 and the threshold check (line 70) are not transactionally guarded against the increment at line 76, so concurrent failed logins can race and overshoot MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS / briefly bypass the lockout boundary (no row lock or atomic compare-and-set).

datetime.utcnow() (lines 90, 105, 147) is deprecated in Python 3.12+ and returns a naive datetime; currently all comparisons are naive-vs-naive and self-consistent so no live bug, but any future mixing with a timezone-aware expires_at would raise TypeError — prefer datetime.now(timezone.utc).
